context.course_content:
Weierstrass Approximation Theorem
Remark 281. This theorem essentially states: "Every continuous function on [a,b] is almost a polynomial."
Theorem 282 (Weierstrass Approximation Theorem)
If f ∈C([a,b]), there exists a sequence of polynomials {P } such that
n
P →f uniformly on [a,b].
n
88

The idea of the proof is to choose a suitable sequence of polynomials {Q } such that Q behaves like a ‘Dirac
n n n
1
delta function’ as nto∞. Then, the sequence of polynomials P (x) = Q (x−t)f(t)dt converges to f(x) as
n 0 n
n→∞. We will prove this momentarily, but first we need to do the ground work.
Notice that we only need to consider a = 0 and b = 1, with f(0) = f(1) = 0. If we prove this case, then for a
general f˜∈C([0,1]), ∃ a sequence of polynomials
P (x)→f˜(x)−f˜(0)−x(f˜(1)−f˜(0)) uniformly.
n
Hence,
P˜ (x)=P (x)+f˜(0)+x(f˜(1)−f˜(0))→f˜(x) uniformly.
n n

Suppose that S ⊂R is nonempty and bounded above. Then, x=supS if and only if
1. x is an upper bound for S.
2. for all >0, ∃y ∈S such that x−<y ≤x.

context.course_content:
Theorem 44
1=sup 1− 1 |n∈N .
n
16

Proof: If n ∈ N, then 1− 1 < 1 =⇒ 1 is an upper bound of this set. Suppose that x is an upper bound for
n
the set {1−1/n | n ∈ N}. We now prove that x ≥ 1. For the sake of contradiction, assume that x < 1. By the
Archimedean property, there exists an n ∈ N such that 1 < n(1−x). Therefore, ∃n ∈ N such that x < 1−1/n.
Hence, x is not an upper bound for the set {1−1/n | n ∈ N} if x < 1. Thus, if x is an upper bound, x ≥ 1.
Therefore,
 
1
sup 1− |n∈N =1.
n
We now begin proving some theorems about supremums and infinimums which will make them easier to use.

Theorem 57 (Cantor)
(0,1] is uncountable.
Proof: We will prove this through contradiction. Suppose that (0,1] is countable. Therefore, there exists a
bijection x:N→(0,1]. We now construct a y ∈(0,1] such that y is not in the range of x. We write
x(n)=0•d(n)d(n)....
−1 −2
These are not exponents! This is the set of digits for a given n ∈ N. In other words, x takes in a natural number
 
n and maps it to the sequence of digits d(n) |n∈N . Let

sincealle sarepositive. Thus,0•e ... istheuniquedecimalexpansionofy. However,foralln∈N,d(n) =e .
−j −1 −n −n
Therefore, ∀n, x(n)=y. This is a contradiction, and thus (0,1] is uncountable.
So (0,1]⊂R is uncountable!

context.course_content:
Here are a few examples and one non-example:
• Z is an ordered set, with the relation that m>n ⇐⇒ m−n∈N.
• Q is an ordered set, with the relation that p>q ⇐⇒ ∃m,n∈N such that p−q = m.
n
• Q×Q is an ordered set with the relation (q,r)>(s,t) ⇐⇒ q >s or q =s and r >t.
• Consider the set P(N). Let A,B ∈ P(N) and let A ≺ B if A ⊂ B. This is NOT an ordered set– it doesn’t
satisfy the first property of an ordered set.
Definition 24 (Bounded Above/Below)
Let S be an ordered set and let E ⊂S. Then,
1. If there exists a b∈S such that x≤b for all x∈E, then E is bounded above and b is an vocab of E.
2. If ∃c∈S such that x≥c for all x∈E, then E is bounded below and c is a lower bound of E.
From here, there are some very important definitions in real analysis. We say that b is the least upper
0
bound, or the supremum of E if
A) b is an upper bound for E and
0
B) if b is an upper bound for E then b ≤b.
0
We denote this as b =supE. Similarly, we say that c is the greatest lower bound, or the infinimum of E if
0 0
A) c is a lower bound for E and
0
B) if c is a lower bound for E then c<c .
0
We denote this as c =infE.
0
10

n
such that x →x.
n
Definition 150 (Function Convergence)
Let S ⊂ R, let c be a cluster point of S, and f : S → R. We say that f(x) converges to L ∈ R at c if ∀ > 0
∃δ >0 such that if x∈S and 0<|x−c|<δ, then |f(x)−L|<.

Theorem 180
If f :[a,b]→R is continuous then f is bounded.
Proof: Suppose for the sake of contradiction that f :[a,b]→R is continuous and f is unbounded. Then, ∀n∈N,
∃x ∈[a,b] such that |f(x )|≥n. By the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem, ∃ a subsequence {x } of {x } and an
n n nk k n n
x∈R such that x →x. Since a≤x ≤b for all k, a≤x≤b. Given f is continuous at x by assumption,
nk nk
f(x)= lim f(x ) =⇒ |f(x)|= lim |f(x )|.
k→∞
nk
k→∞
nk
Therefore, {|f(x )|} is bounded, and thus {n } is bounded since n ≤ |f(x )|. But by the definition of a
nk k k nk
subsequence, we must have k ≤n for all k, contradicting the boundedness of {n }.
k k
Definition 181 (Absolute Minimum/Maximum)
Let f : S → R. Then, f achieves an absolute minimum at c if ∀x ∈ S, f(x) ≥ f(c). Similarly, f achieves an
absolute maximum at d if ∀x∈S, f(x)≤f(d).
Theorem 182 (Min-Max Theorem)
Let f :[a,b]→R. If f is continuous, then f achieves an absolute maximum and absolute minimum.
Remark 183. Note that this is also called the Extreme Value Theorem or EVT for short, though to stay consistent
with the Lebl’s book I will be calling it the Min-Max theorem.
Proof: We will prove this for the absolute maximum. If f is continuous, then f is bounded by the previous
theorem. Thus, the set
E ={f(x)|x∈[a,b]}
56

is bounded above. Let L=supE. Then,
1. L is an upper bound for E, i.e.
∀x∈[a,b], f(x)≤L.
2. There exists a sequence {f(x )} with x ∈[a,b] such that f(x )→L.
n n n n
By the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem, there exists a subsequence {x } of {x } and d ∈ [a,b] such that x → d
nk k n nk
as k →∞. Hence,
f(d)= lim f(x )= lim f(x )=L
k→∞
nk
n→∞
n
by the continuity of f. Thus, f achieves an absolute maximum at d.
We leave the absolute minimum proof to the reader.
Remark 184. As students of mathematics, we also care about the necessity of the hypotheses!
For example, what if f : [a,b] → R is not continuous? Does the Min-Max theorem apply? The answer is no.
